Event: Kurt kicks well if he does the "Single Ladies" dance before games
Explanation: He discovers and uses a quirky routine that improves his performance, helping both himself and the team.

Event: Kurt openly aspires to Broadway
Explanation: Pursuing a clear artistic goal benefits his own future and adds value to others if achieved.

Event: Finn reprimands Puck for pushing Kurt
Explanation: He risks some social friction to stop bullying, modestly helping himself morally and clearly helping Kurt.

Event: Kurt flies into fury when friends (Tina/Blaine) are abused
Explanation: His anger is protective of others and affirms his own boundaries despite some emotional cost.

Event: Kurt and Blaine share a Big Damn Kiss
Explanation: Mutual romantic expression strengthens their relationship and harms no one.

Event: Kurt wants to hold his comatose father’s hand
Explanation: Offering comfort to his father also soothes Kurt emotionally.

Event: Kurt and Blaine hold hands after their relationship upgrade
Explanation: Affectionate intimacy nurtures both their emotional well-being.

Event: Showrunners increase Kurt’s screen time
Explanation: They respond to fan interest, boosting Kurt’s prominence and audience satisfaction.

Event: Kurt stops manipulative scheming after it backfires
Explanation: Abandoning tactics that harmed him and strained others yields better relationships.

Event: Glee club rallies around Kurt during harassment
Explanation: Their support moderately costs them but greatly helps Kurt’s safety and morale.

Event: Kurt becomes friends with former bullies
Explanation: Forging friendships with past tormentors improves his environment and their growth.

Event: Kurt becomes confidant to Sam over his homelessness
Explanation: He sacrifices some time and secrecy to materially and emotionally help Sam.

Event: Kurt gives Sam spare clothes
Explanation: Material and emotional aid costs Kurt little and strongly benefits Sam.

Event: Kurt keeps Sam’s secret under duress
Explanation: Protecting Sam’s dignity under pressure mildly risks Kurt but greatly helps Sam.

Event: Burt takes Kurt’s cool car away
Explanation: Neutral disciplinary move; no clear long-term harm or gain is specified.

Event: Kurt removes Finn’s jacket before dumpster toss
Explanation: He protects Finn’s expensive jacket at minor risk to himself and no one is harmed.

Event: Kurt emulates Burt’s masculine persona out of fear
Explanation: His self-erasure hurts his own authenticity while only slightly easing perceived tension with Burt.

Event: Kurt gradually accepts and shows off his physique
Explanation: Self-acceptance boosts his confidence and clarifies equality in the relationship.

Event: Kurt refuses to apologize for not being a “delicate flower”
Explanation: He asserts healthy independence, improving his self-respect and pushing Blaine to adjust expectations.

Event: Kurt’s communication mode is brutal honesty
Explanation: Honesty helps him and, despite occasional sting, tends to clarify truths for others.

Event: Kurt chooses “Mister Cellophane” as an audition song
Explanation: He picks a characterful piece that suits his persona, helping his performance with no stated harm.

Event: Kurt creates a Lady Gaga-inspired costume and performs “Bad Romance”
Explanation: Expressive performance boosts his confidence and entertains others.

Event: Kurt tries to emulate a stereotypical “straight boy” and date Brittany
Explanation: He harms his own authenticity and confuses Brittany while only superficially testing an argument.

Event: Kurt removes layers to show his new physique while narrating past insecurity
Explanation: He embraces growth and self-acceptance, modeling it positively for others.

Event: Kurt uses his catchphrase “Mr. Schue, if I may?”
Explanation: Asserting a polite voice in class benefits his agency and slightly enriches discussion.

Event: Kurt forgives former bullies and is friendly with them
Explanation: Forgiveness improves his social environment and supports their reform.

Event: Kurt appears cross-legged as a habitual tic
Explanation: Neutral comfort behavior with negligible, perhaps mildly positive, self-expression impact.

Event: Kurt acts as closet key to Karofsky
Explanation: Helping someone out of the closet helps Kurt socially but creates stressful pressure for Karofsky.

Event: Kurt helps Sam by listening and advising in “Rumours”
Explanation: Emotional labor mildly costs him and clearly supports Sam’s resilience.

Event: Kurt deadpan-snarks at Rachel
Explanation: His snark indulges his wit but often undercuts Rachel and offers her little benefit.

Event: Kurt drops his haughty front when he trusts someone
Explanation: Vulnerability deepens his relationships, helping everyone involved.

Event: Kurt wears women’s clothing and defends “fashion has no gender”
Explanation: Authentic self-expression bolsters his confidence and challenges others’ norms constructively.

Event: Kurt is easily forgiven for scheming and harassment
Explanation: He escapes much consequence for actions that previously harmed others.

Event: Kurt tacitly apologizes by giving up his duet with Sam
Explanation: Surrendering a musical opportunity to mend relationships helps others and modestly redeems Kurt.

Event: Kurt breaks off his engagement to Blaine coldly
Explanation: He preserves his own autonomy at the severe emotional and academic cost to Blaine.

Event: Blaine immediately forgives Kurt after deep hurt
Explanation: His instant forgiveness soothes Kurt but leaves Blaine’s own pain unaddressed.

Event: Kurt moves to NYC to re-audition for NYADA
Explanation: Pursuing a second chance advances his career and future contributions.

Event: Kurt surprises himself by auditioning at NYADA Winter Showcase
Explanation: His risk yields admission and acclaim, benefiting his path and delighting the audience.

Event: Kurt as fashionista curates elaborate wardrobe
Explanation: His styling boosts his self-image and aesthetically pleases others.

Event: Kurt joins Home Economics as the only boy
Explanation: He gains skills and normalizes gender-role flexibility for others.

Event: Kurt plays football and works comfortably on cars with Burt
Explanation: Shared activities strengthen his skills and deepen his bond with his father.

Event: Kurt prepares two complex NYADA audition routines
Explanation: He invests enormous effort that is rejected while still entertaining and impressing others.

Event: Kurt switches mid-audition from “Music of the Night” to “Not the Boy Next Door”
Explanation: On-the-spot risk showcases authenticity and talent, impressing the room despite later rejection.

Event: Kurt attempts to act straight and date Brittany to “test” his sexuality
Explanation: This self-denial causes confusion and discomfort without real benefit.

Event: Burt states he knew Kurt was gay since age three
Explanation: Open acceptance supports Kurt’s identity and security.

Event: Kurt initially insists Blaine is not his boyfriend while admitting attraction
Explanation: Cautious honesty balances his feelings and Blaine’s autonomy without harm.

Event: Kurt and Blaine become an official couple
Explanation: Mutual commitment greatly enriches both and harms nobody.

Event: Kurt returns from Dalton to McKinley
Explanation: Coming back to his old school restores his social network and bolsters the club.

Event: Kurt reads Sun Tzu and develops hidden depths
Explanation: His broad interests enrich his mind and occasionally aid others.

Event: Kurt is portrayed as demure and shorter than Blaine in media photos
Explanation: Marketing benefits from a constructed dynamic that slightly distorts Kurt’s perceived persona.

Event: Kurt comes out of the closet and lives as an out gay man
Explanation: Authenticity and visibility powerfully help him and provide representation for others.

Event: Kurt tries to seduce straight Finn via manipulation
Explanation: He chases an impossible crush at Finn’s expense, briefly feeding his fantasy while harming Finn.

Event: Kurt tries to turn Finn off girls by calling them catty and troublesome
Explanation: He undermines Finn’s relationships to increase his own chances.

Event: Kurt maneuvers so Finn moves into his bedroom
Explanation: He gains physical proximity while making Finn uncomfortable and breaching boundaries.

Event: Kurt makes a subtler romantic attempt on Sam
Explanation: He again pursues a likely straight boy, risking Sam’s ease for his own hopes.

Event: Kurt openly disdains others’ fashion while upset at being picked on
Explanation: His defensive snobbery makes others targets while not actually reducing his own victimization.

Event: Kurt expresses disdain for bisexuality
Explanation: His biphobia harms and alienates others while providing no real benefit to him.

Event: Kurt favors female roles and gets angry when forced into male roles
Explanation: He advances his preferences at the expense of fair role distribution.

Event: Kurt tells Finn that girls are catty to steer him away from them
Explanation: He manipulates Finn’s perceptions of women in service of his crush.

Event: Kurt dismisses the existence of bisexuality to Blaine
Explanation: His invalidation hurts Blaine’s self-exploration and undermines trust without helping Kurt.

Event: Kurt brings Blaine flowers after Blaine wins the part Kurt wanted
Explanation: He swallows disappointment to celebrate Blaine, deepening their relationship.

Event: Kurt rudely interrupts Santana’s proposal to condemn young marriage
Explanation: He vents his own bitterness and publicly spoils their moment without gaining anything.

Event: Kurt sings “Rose’s Turn” as an “I Want” song
Explanation: Expressing his desires clarifies his goals and resonates with listeners.

Event: Kurt threatens to punch the next person who calls him a stereotype
Explanation: Setting a firm boundary protects his dignity, with only minor social friction.

Event: Kurt and Blaine behave like an old married couple
Explanation: Their stable dynamic supports mutual emotional security and comforts friends observing it.

Event: Kurt befriends Brittany and dances “Single Ladies” with her
Explanation: Their odd friendship gives them joy and cross-clique connection.

Event: Kurt slowly becomes close friends and roommates with Rachel (and Santana)
Explanation: Co-living and friendship strengthen all their support systems.

Event: Kurt and Mercedes form a platonic life-partnership
Explanation: Deep platonic support massively benefits both with no downside.

Event: Kurt fashions and wears a kilt to prom
Explanation: Expressive choice boosts his confidence and broadens others’ ideas of masculinity.

Event: Someone rigs prom vote to make Kurt Prom Queen
Explanation: The rigger gains amusement while cruelly humiliating Kurt.

Event: Kurt turns the Prom Queen prank around and owns it proudly
Explanation: He converts intended humiliation into empowerment, inspiring others.

Event: Kurt persuades Holly Holliday to teach Glee using puppy-dog eyes
Explanation: His persuasion secures a fun, skilled teacher, benefiting everyone.

Event: Kurt plays football while being camp and into fashion
Explanation: Blending roles strengthens his confidence and challenges stereotypes, aiding others.

Event: Sue demands Kurt perform in drag at Nationals
Explanation: It gives Sue a striking act while pressuring and objectifying Kurt.

Event: Kurt is repeatedly tossed into dumpsters
Explanation: They expend effort to torment him without any real gain and risk consequences.

Event: Kurt grabs the slushie and throws it in his own face to spare Finn
Explanation: He harms himself to protect Finn’s conscience and social standing.

Event: Kurt steals Sue’s “Physical” video from her locked cabinet
Explanation: He gains leverage or amusement by violating Sue’s privacy and potentially embarrassing her.

Event: Burt calls Kurt out for inappropriate behavior toward Finn
Explanation: His criticism corrects Kurt’s boundary issues and guides him toward healthier conduct.

Event: Everyone calls Kurt out for denying bisexuality
Explanation: Social pushback corrects his harmful bias, improving his understanding and how he treats others.

Event: Blaine compares Kurt’s biphobia to Karofsky’s bigotry
Explanation: Harsh but accurate parallel shocks Kurt toward self-reflection and better behavior.

Event: Blaine worries Kurt’s romanticized view of sex will make him vulnerable
Explanation: His concern prompts healthier sexual awareness, helping Kurt’s safety.

Event: Kurt and Blaine eventually have sex and develop an active sex life
Explanation: Mutual, desired intimacy strengthens their bond and fulfillment.

Event: Burt consistently supports Kurt’s identity and assures him he loves him as he is
Explanation: Unconditional acceptance maximally benefits Kurt and fosters a healthy model for others.
